I went back to the seller and they had quit selling them because of this problem; it took less than a year for moderate to heavy users which meant alot of warranty replacements.

Brand: Sony Aspects: build, comfort, sound Rating: 1.0 Importance Score: 15.51 Review: manufacturing defects I bought two of these headphones. Both had serious manufacturing defects.

On one, the supplied RCA cord was bad (no sound at all). I replaced this with one from my junk box and all was well.

The other had to be returned. (Aside, one reason I buy from Amazon is their excellent return polices). There simply was no sound to the left earpiece.

While a population of two, of course, is insufficient to make a lasting judgement, it does seem the factory fails the soldering/crimping test. :)

The purpose of these was simply to allow my wife to watch/listen to TV in the middle of the night without awaking me. (one for the small bedroom TV and the other for the monster in the living room). Hence the absence of comments on sound quality.. except to set them up (or try to), I have never had them on.

Brand: Bose Aspects: battery, build, comfort, connectivity, microphone, price, sound Rating: 2.0 Importance Score: 10.78 Review: Return! I returned for the following reasons
1) does not block wind. The 3 pairs of cheap $30 headphones I've had for 6 years blocked wind. I'm not sure if it's because the speakers stick out far, or the rubber ear does not insert as tightly, but there were parts of my walk outside that I couldn't hear a podcast because of the wind, which wasn't even that strong.
2) No magnetic attachment. When I have my headphones not on I like them to be magnetically clasped around my neck. The alligator clip and weight of the headphones helped some, but not enough
3) Battery life is too low. Previous headphones I've worn had a 9 hour battery life, which meant very sporadic charging. After a few days I felt the need to charge these every day.

I did like
1) sound quality
2) quick connection via bluetooth
3) easy turn on off with an easy press
4) I can put my phone down and walk away and it wouldn't drop.

Brand: Sony Aspects: anc, battery, comfort, connectivity, sound Rating: 2.0 Importance Score: 19.89 Review: I really want to like these... This is my first purchase of earbuds in the over $200 category. I got the Jabra Elite 65t as soon as they came out and have since bought a couple of other pairs of earbuds in the under $100 category that had better features like IPX7 or 8hr battery life. Therefore I was excited to upgrade to the XM4 for their bettery life and the noise canceling.

Unfortunately, I've had several issues with these. I don't have particularly small ears. Usually the medium tips fit without issue. On the XM4, I started with the medium tips and tested their air tightness using the app. The small tips don't have any kind of seal on my ears without really shoving them super far down my ear canal, so I decided to stick with the medium size. Unfortunately, I have experienced significant ear fatigue enough so that I can't wear the earbuds for more than a couple of hours. I have since switched to the small tips for the sake of comfort, but that means sacrificing any passive noise canceling - and unless I have a defective unit, without the passive noise canceling in addition to the active noise canceling, there is absolutely no difference between having the noise canceling on and off. To me, this completely defeats the purpose of buying noise canceling earbuds.

I also have had issues with connectivity such as the bluetooth connection on my phone suddenly dropping and having to go into the app to manually pair them again.

In terms of battery life, depending on the control settings of each earbud, there can be a difference in the drain of battery between the left and right side. On a bad day, I had a 30% difference with the right draining significantly faster. My earbuds are using the factory settings with the playback controls on the right and the noise canceling on the left for reference.

Both the connectivity problems and the bettery difference between the earbuds has seemed to have gotten better since performing a factory reset, but be aware that there is still a little difference in the rate of battery drain between the L and R earbuds (It's not super significant now and maybe reaches under 7-8% difference at the end of the day).

I bought some Comply foam tips, which I hope will fix the discomfort issue and make the noise canceling work at the optimal rate without the ear fatigue. However, I shouldn't have to modify almost $300 earbuds to make them work like they should. It would help if Sony would offer more sizes of ear tips to help make them more usable for a wider population. Then again, if I have gripes like these, then maybe the XM4s aren't for me. Makes me super sad considering I think that they're probably fantastic for most people.

Brand: Bose Aspects: anc, battery, build, comfort, connectivity, microphone, price, sound Rating: 2.0 Importance Score: 14.07 Review: Still hear the Demons This review is for people that care about secure fit/comfort, sound quality (loudness), battery-charging/durability and quality/convenience (protrusion, physical stability, size). If you are concerned of whether or not these are sweat-proof, "look cool", have "sleek packaging" or "easy to pair", then go read the 5000 other reviews that care about these types of non-critical-never-a-problem-time wasting minutia.

I need earbuds that will stay in your ear during physical activity (running) and will fit inside my motorcycle helmet that will cancel out background sound and are loud enough to block out the goblins in my mind. I have yet to find a pair of wireless earbuds that meet my requirements.

Secure Fit/Comfort- Terrible: These things are bulky and heavy. Although, the ear-fins keep them "steady" none of the rubber ear pieces (includes 3) make a tight fit. This ruins the sound quality (allows too much ambient sound) and undermines the entire product. Their weight also puts odd pressure on your inner ear which results in discomfort/pain. I bought a motorcycle helmet that is Xlarge so I would have room to stuff earbuds in my ears and then cram my melon into the helmet. If you jack your head around and pull-push-squeeze-cram you can eventually get your head into the helmet with these buds still-in your ears but, it's all for nothing because as soon as you get moving the wind-ambient sound all-but ruins the volume/quality.

Sound Quality-Average: Since much of the sound quality is dependent on the source; Samsung LGG3-Pandora. If you turn everything up to it's maximum levels and are sitting on your butt on a couch with no other outside noises...they sound average. Considering the cost of these earbuds drops them to below average.

Battery charging, durability, quality -Good: They charge with a standard USB/micro connection (no special adapter needed) and they feel heavy and well built. This is probably the only good aspect of this product.

My current pair are the JayBird BlueBuds X Sport and they are the only pair thus far that meet my particular needs. Unfortunately, the battery is near death (3 years old) and these BlueBuds still do not stay securely in place while exercising and/or stuffed into a helmet. Although, they do a much better job at sealing out ambient noise, I had hoped that after 3 years somebody (Bose, JayBird, Beats ?) would have elevated/advanced the most critical elements of wireless earbuds but, apparently not. We still read lame reviews of earbuds that address shipping/packaging, pairing (even the cheapest Bluetooth electronic pairs easily) and aesthetics (who cares what color, style, shape...just make them loud and stay secure!). I guess I'll go try and find another pair of BlueBuds and maybe crazy glue-silicone the ear-tips permanently in my ear and finally quiet the demons in my head

Brand: Sony Aspects: anc, comfort, connectivity, microphone, price, sound Rating: 1.0 Importance Score: 40.46 Review: NOT an "over-ear" headphone... "Over-ear" is a very poorly chosen term because something "over" your ear might also be considered to be something "on" your ear and vice versa. The proper term that should have been established for use is "around-ear" or "surround-ear" and, in fact, Sennheiser uses the term "around-ear" on their website to describe their headphones which are otherwise listed with the "over-ear" misnomer.

I wonder if the reason the term "over-ear" became the default term was because those who established it were not native English speakers who didn't realize the nuances. Or, perhaps, it was just some native English speaking idiot in marketing.

Anyway, unlike the unambiguously named "on-ear" headphones, "over-ear" headphones are supposed to surround your ears with the pads resting on your skull and not on your ears. With poorly designed versions of those, like some of those which use memory foam in the ear pads, sweating can be an issue. With properly designed ones, it isn't.

What characteristics are required for a proper "over-ear," better called "around-ear" headphone? These:

1. The holes in the center of the headphone pads should be large enough for your ears to fit inside.
2. The headphone pads should be thick enough to completely raise the hard headphone driver (speaker) face away from your ears OR
3. A combination of adequate headphone pad thickness and a rearward sloping of the driver away from the head to prevent contact with the ears which, as you may have noticed, slope away from your head in the rearward direction.

These headphones as well as a previous pair of a different brand that I bought at a similar price do not comply with any of those characteristics. As a further insult, to make up for their too-thin ear pads, they're too rigid in order to reduce the amount they collapse.

Have headphone manufacturers somehow forgotten those required characteristics over years? No, I suspect they are simply cost cutting to compete with Chinese manufacturers and smaller headphones are more stylish and more easily stored for travel.

They suffer little negative market reaction for doing this because most people apparently don't know what an "over-ear" headphone is supposed to be. They put the headphones on and if they work, sound OK, and aren't immediately uncomfortable, give them positive reviews. Also, some reviewers may have very small ears where the headphones actually do act as a "surround-ear" design although with this model and the previous one I also returned, I don't see that as being possible for anyone over 6 years old.

Brand: Sony Aspects: anc, battery, comfort, connectivity, price, sound Rating: 2.0 Importance Score: 19.91 Review: Disappointing for the price If this was a $20 headphone, its sin could be forgiven, but with a price of over $100, one cannot excuse a wireless headphone that keeps on clicking and making reconnecting sounds even if you just slightly move your head. I'm getting this while being near the transmitter and within line of sight. I tried the various settings on the transmitter and the auto-connect on the headset, none of them made a difference. I don't know if Wifi or smartphone or other technology is causing interference, but I'm not going to shutdown everything every time I want to use the RF headphones.

At the same spots, the generic As Seen On TV headsets you'd find in stores did not have an issue maintaining a connection. They had all kinds of other issues, but the sound is kinda important in a headset.

I do not recommend them at the current $100+ price. If they go on clearance for 1/3rd or less, assuming you can ignore or survive the clicking / reconnecting sounds, everything else about them is good. They have solid construction, the headset can use standard AAA batteries if the included battery pack runs out while you are in the middle of a movie or a TV show, there is a tactile volume wheel on the headset, meaningful lights on the base and the headset.

The base (the transmitter part that connects to your audio source) has to be connected to AC power all the time. There is no battery option for the base. The headset comes with a removable battery pack that charges when you rest the headset on the base. Or you can use your own two AAA batteries.

Charging takes a while (an overnight task or day-long when you are away from them). The base has a red light on while charging and it turns off after charging is complete. Battery life is very good, I never had them run out during a single day. I don't recharge them every night, I wait until I have a ballpark of 6-8 hours or so (I don't want them to run out in the middle of the next time I use).

Input is a standard 3.5mm audio port (1/8"). If you want to connect an RCA source, you have to use your own adapter (they are cheap and easy to find but it would have been nice if Sony included one to make it easier for new buyers). A 3.5mm audio cable is included or you can use your own if you prefer a longer or shorter one.

The headset has a power on/off button. The base does not. It activates when sound is playing and it has a blinking green light. Once the headset is turned on and connected, it is a solid green light. The lights are not annoyingly bright.

Brand: Sony Aspects: anc, battery, build, comfort, connectivity, microphone, sound Rating: 1.0 Importance Score: 18.42 Review: DO NOT BUY! Horrible distortion There is a lot to like about these buds. Sadly they have an extremely bad flaw which is not getting enough attention by Sony.

They have horrible "tinny" or "metallic" echo, commonly heard in the higher vocal range. This is most noticeable on calls, and podcasts. It's impossible to ignore.

Amazon will not allow me to post a link, but you can search around and find people complaining, there is a good thread on their forum. Search for "Sony WF-1000XM4 metallic echo".

This happens on ANC and. non-ANC modes. Using "hear through" does make it less noticeable.

By the time I noticed this, the return window was closed (didn't use them much for the first month). I have since returned two units to Sony for repair. The second set was even worse than the first, which leads me to think it might be a manufacture defect, rather than a design issue.

I will update the review if this gets resolved. Meanwhile, stay away from these!

The good:
- comfortable for IMEs
- excellent battery life
- when distortion is not present (see above), reasonable sound quality
- good ANC
- a snowballs chance of replacing the battery in a year so they are not just e-waste
- decent app

The bad:
- distortion (see above)
- capacitive touch controls are a bit annoying; prefer the tactile versions on some other deigns
- app has some gimmicks that are not that useful

Brand: Bose Aspects: battery, comfort, connectivity, microphone, price, sound Rating: 1.0 Importance Score: 19.85 Review: Degrades quickly, irritating, and Bluetooth is garbage 7 Month Update: Knocking off the stars I gave before, because the cable sheath has already fallen apart (Bose, you've been screwing this up for at least eight years of revisions - fix this already!), the microphone doesn't work anymore, and the battery life is now compete garbage (3 hours on a good day).

Additionally, the low battery warning is obnoxious, long, frequent, and starts too early. It mutes what you're listening to, and the voice says "Battery low. Please charge now." I know that "battery low" means it needs charging, and you don't have to talk over my podcast every two minutes to remind me and reinform me that it needs to be charged. I will not charge now - I will use it until either the battery dies or I get to a point where it's convenient to charge. The only thing the stupid voice accomplishes is to irritate me.

After eight years of Bose headphones, it seems likely this will be my last - they clearly have trouble learning from their mistakes, and they've made many with this model. Not even remotely worth the premium price tag.

Original review :
I imagine that my disappointment has a lot to do with Bluetooth or the limitations of being wireless, but coming from the simplicity of wired headphones, these have been driving me up the wall. The five or so hours of battery life I get doesn't come close to what I need from it. The voice fails miserably at pronouncing the names of all of my devices (though I guess we can't blame any technology for the ambiguity that is English pronunciation). The new sound blocking earpieces hurt my ears (coming from the old style, without the cone), though with the old ones on, it doesn't get loud enough (and would probably eat the battery if it did). The wire casing is pretty concerning for longevity, but this has always been a problem with Bose's earbuds; I'll probably wind up reinforcing it with some macrame like I did with my old wired ones.

Maybe it deserves a fourth star, given what it is, but one star off for the troubles wrought by this path in technology we're taking.

Brand: Audio-Technica Aspects: anc, build, comfort, price, sound Rating: 5.0 Importance Score: 5.26 Review: Long Term Experience Review (4+ Years) In December 2014, I bought a limited edition version of the A-T 50x headphones. Wired, with a sort of tan, almost copper color. They are now very unique. I like them partly for that uniqueness in appearance. However, where they really shine is in their audio quality.

Purchased in December 2014, I've used these DAILY (and I mean 4-10 hours a day) for well over 4 years. Yes, folks that is nearly 4-1/2 years of ownership. They still sound fantastic. No loss in audio quality. No damaged cords. The one thing I've had to deal with is replacing the cups (the earphone cushions). And you know, considering the OEM ones worked well for 4 years, I'm good with that.

I cannot overstate how incredible these headphones are in terms of audio quality. I have a collection of headphones - mostly wireless - for listening to music and gaming on PC, and occasionaly listening to music on a smart phone (brand has varied over the years).

The Audio-Technica ATH-M50x is HANDS DOWN my go-to headphones for any application where I am demanding the best audio quality in a set of headphones. Now, are there better headphones out there? Arguably, yes, of course. But I would bet anyone that up to the $250 price range, Audio-Technica is hard to beat. I don't believe you can do it. They are that good. I know that sounds cliche, but if you read through all the reviews on the M50x or its sister products, you'll find I'm not the only person who has drunk the Audio-Technica Kool-Aid.

Bottom line: run, don't walk to the checkout line! These are the real deal. Old school Japanese heritage in electronics. I can hardly believe any electronic device this well made still exists today, but here it is; for $150. There is a God!

Brand: Sony Aspects: anc, comfort, connectivity, microphone, price, sound Rating: 5.0 Importance Score: 7.59 Review: Beyond anything I could have imagined...if noise distracts, buy back your productivity! I have used active noise canceling technology in protective ear muffs, and I have had some ear buds that were passive noise canceling, so I kind of thought I had an idea what these where going to be like. Nothing could prepare me for my first impression, and we’ll every subsequent impression.

My wife does telemedicine from home, and soon we plan to add another dedicated office, but for right now we share a room. The distraction of having a constant conversation in the background has driven me to feel like a nomad - wandering from flat spot to flat spot. The laptop isn’t an issue, but the scanner, 32” monitor, keyboard, keypad, paperwork,etc...well you get the point. So I found a 17” led up in the attic...no go, for the record don’t ever get used to one size monitor than essentially try to get use to one 1/4th that size. I tried listening to music with my passive ear buds, finally I used my protective muffs that I have for mowing, shooting, anything loud and it sort of worked but those try to defeat harmful noise levels. I tried wearing earbuds playing music underneath the muffs and while that worked, my ears however felt like they had been in a vice.

Well that leads me to these, while these are more costly than anything I ever have purchased for audio - other than my home theater equipment. Let me say now I’m case I am being wordy, these are worth every penny.

I can be attached to both my iPhone and laptop, so I can get feedback from my computer or listen to audio and still here when I get an alert on my phone. When I wish, the head phones do not require any actual music or anything. I literally feel like I am alone in a “cone of silence” for those familiar with the old Get Smart episodes. If my wife or any other family member needs me it is easier to text me than to call out to me. While that is a little inconvenient, my ability to concentrate has increased multi-fold which has also allowed my productivity to feeling a pure joy rather than the stress of always feeling like you need to do more.

These are also light, and have no issue navigating by size 8 head. The app setup for these is wild. I have done moldable ear plugs, but I never dreamed I would take a selfie and then snap shots of each ear to customize the sound. The other fascinating thing happened completely by accident, I was listening to a presentation online but needed to work on dinner. I thought I paused the pc and joined on the phone. But evidently I never stopped the pc and hearing audio I didn’t worry about anything. Well I realized that my pc was communicating with the Sony app and the app was evidently acting like a radio repeater...I was out of range of my office but set my phone out of the way where it won’t get wet, dropped or anything else. It was only when I tried to stop the presentation did I realize what had occurred. I now use this all the time and have subsequently learned to pause/play with the controls. Lastly, and this will completely give away the secret that I never read instructions...I could not figure out how to connect a phone call. I used the buttons like I do on my other Bluetooth earbuds, but that didn’t work. I felt stupid when I learned all that I had to do was tap the center of the right muff.

I know this is a lot of money, and While it is rare for productivity be literally monetized unless you are on commission, I can tell you that these allow for peace in total chaos...oh, if I had these 20 years ago when our second child was colicky🙂.

I normally buy black by default, grey if black isn’t available, but my wife encouraged me to try the blue. While I am color blind, I still see color, amd like the blue...my wife also likes the color which is way more important. I am starting to sense that I will be buying another pair of these for my wife because when she is charting on her EMR distractions are awful.

Brand: JVC Aspects: anc, battery, build, comfort, connectivity, microphone, price, sound Rating: 4.0 Importance Score: 26.83 Review: detailed analysis: 3.5 stars: Decent for the price point! 3.5 stars taking price into account

With headphones, it is always a blend of "you get what you paid for", and comparing to others at a similar price point.
How do these compare? Well, they are about average for the $20-$40 range. Not much better, not much worse. And as is the case with headphones, for a bit more, you can do MUCH better, and for a bit less, you will get roughly similar results. (In other words, headphones under $75-$100 are, more or less very similar, and then suddenly, your options get drastically better.)

Here's my analysis on the general sound quality and other features:

MISC:
Phone calls: 5 stars: Easy to use, works great for calls on both ends, and blocks outside sound quite well!
Ease of use/pairing: 5 stars: It passed the test of not having to read instructions, meaning it is incredibly simple to pair and use
Appearance: 5 stars: Neat! It's colorful and fun, as well as practical and functional!
External noise: 4 stars: Because it has a pretty tight seal, it is like an earplug, blocking outside sound quite effectively
Comfort: 3 stars: comparable to others of this design. Totally fine at first, and annoying after long use.

SOUND:
Treble: 2 stars: quite tinny
Bass: 2 stars: ok depth, not great, not very powerful
Overall: 2 stars: not much detail in the sound, but it can get pretty loud without distorting, and it is definitely good enough for basic listening and phone calls. There also appears to be a *very slight* hiss in quiet/silent moments when the volume is louder.

OVERALL:
The average is 28 out of 40 possible points, or 70% (a "C" grade, if it were going to school ;-), but because the above analysis does not take price point into account, comparing this to brands like Bose, as well as dollar store headphones, this means that for the current list price of roughly $29.99, this is a reasonable expectation.

The average number of stars based on my rubric is 3.5 stars: decent for this price point, and certainly good enough to enjoy music and spoken word, and to have successful phone calls.

Review: Comfortable, Good Sound Quality, Unsurpassed Noise Cancelling I had been looking for a headset to use with my TV so I could watch at night without disturbing anyone had have good sound as well. I had been using my son's LilGadgets Untangled Pro Premium Children's Wireless Bluetooth Headphones with SharePort - Black.  They sounded good but would become uncomfortable as they were an on hear headset designed and sized for kids.  I wanted something that was comfortable, sounded good, and was an over the hear design. The headset had to have the ability to sync with the TV.  I first tried the Bose QuietComfort 35 first.  They were comfortable but kept losing connection, didn't sync well with the TV voice, and had a rather bland sound. This was compared to my son's sons headset listed above. I was so disappointed and returned the Bose 35. My next headset was the Beats by Dr. Dre - Beats Studio Wireless Over-the-Ear Headphones - Black. This headset was comfortable, sounded great, but had some issues with syncing with the TV. I was reluctant to purchase the Sony MDR1000X headset but gave it a try.  They are comfortable but can get a bit hot to wear for an extended time. I found the sound quality to be best of the units I tried. The Studio headset was close and seemed to have a bit more Bass but I kept having sync issues. Only the Sony MDR1000X has Apt-X and when used with an Apt-X Blu Tooth transmitter I never have sync issues when watching TV programs. I was comparing the Sony MDR1000X and Studio while watching the same programs and scenes.  As mentioned the sound quality was close and I liked the extra Bass on the Studio. The Studio was also less expensive than the Sony. I decided I wanted to ensure I would not have lip sync issues and choose the Sony over the Studio.  I use the Sony headset daily and have found they are comfortable to wear for hours but can get a bit hot and have to remove them for a bit to cool off.  The battery lasts as advertised and I can go a week on one full charge. The sound quality is good and stereo separation is great. For TV viewing you can hear background sounds not present when watch TV through the TV speakers! The sound canceling is excellent! This can be good or bad. The good I only hear what I am listening too. The bad I am not able to hear my young son if he wakes up with a bad dream. For me the volume control works flawlessly and it is much better than having to find a button or rocker switch. The supplied cables and case are excellent and much better than the other headset listed in this review. For me the Sony does everything I wanted from a headset for listening to TV. They are expensive but have a better build quality than the other headset and it is my hope they will last. I leave the sound canceling on full but you adjust it for voice and ambient noise. One thing I can not get to work is the control functions.  I don't expect it to work with my TV using an external Blu Tooth transmitter but I did expect it to work with my Samsung Galaxy S7 Edge.  It does not.  I also cannot connect the Sony headset via Blue Tooth to my Fire TV. I could do so with all the other headsets. I am not giving this headset five stars for some connection Blu Tooth connection limitations and that they get hot while wearing. They are the best of the group I tested against for what I use them for.  I could have continued using my sons $49 Lilgadgets but wanted a bit more and the ability to wear them for more than two hours at a time. I would recommend them but I will add you need to check them out yourself. Everyone's head is different and a headsets comfort level is going to be individual. I believe sound quality is also subjective and will be up to the individual to determine what sound presents they prefer.

There was a Sony Style store that was closing so they were selling their products at a significant discount. I am the type of person who tries before buying, but this particular headphone was not available for demo, nor did they allow me to open the box because of the closeout. Still, I was a little suspicious because I had a pair of Panasonic noise canceling headphones that did not work for jack! I had just visited the Bose store and tried the new QC15 noise canceling headphones--they were awesome, but for nearly $300 bucks, yougottabekiddin me.

My commute each day involving bus, metro, walking, and driving can take up to 5 hours, so I needed a pair of noise-isolating or noise-canceling headphones to reduce the outside noise so I could listen to my music and audiobooks in peace.

After about an hour of hemming and hawing about whether I should buy them from the Sony store because of the "all sales are final" aspect, I relented and immediately rushed home to try them out.

Well, let me tell you, after installing the AA battery (which was provided, thanks Sony), I turned them on and--wala, no difference in sound or noise reduction. I walked over and turned on the bathroom vent and played with the on/off switch several times, but again I didn't notice any appreciable difference. I went to our laundry room and the dryer was running, and again playing with the switch to see if there was any reduction in noise. Nope! I was robbed! I immediately called Sony Style customer support and demanded a refund or replacement with another product. They said they couldn't help me because the customer service department was closed and they'd have them call me later in the week. I didn't like that answer so I stewed and remained content for the time being. Since I had a few days before I gave them my what fer, I decided to bring these headphones along with me on my commute and see if they actually worked.

At o-dark-thirty in the morning, I boarded my bus and first tried these on without the noise canceling on. They were very similar to your average ear bud headphones, but certainly better than the standard Apple earbuds. I have a pair of Westone UM-1 earphones which I considered one of the better noise isolating headphones, and they did a much better job at reducing noise. Well, that changed after I turned on the switch. It took about 2-3 seconds for the system to work, but lo-and-behold, after the microphones, which are located at the top of the earbuds, began to capture the ambient bus noise, the noise canceling actually started to work!

The areas that these headphones isolate the best are in the steady low frequency noises emitted by buses, trains, and airplanes. They don't do such a good job at mid or high frequency noises. But those noises are reduced by the rubber ear tips with the good seal they create. The low frequency noises are reduced by the noise canceling feature of these headphones.

Needless to say I was impressed!

These earbuds come with the standard Sony style of being left-side biased, meaning that the earbuds connect on the left side vice forming a y in the middle of your body like most other ear buds. This is a kind of quirky design and I haven't yet gotten used to it. But it makes for easy identification of the ear pieces especially in the dark. The cables are a little more robust than their Fontopia headphones, but not as sturdy as Shure's earphones or the great flat design found in the Monster/Dr. Dre ear bud line. The sound is about average for a product like this. The mids and lows are a little muddy and the highs are not as bright as other more expensive earphones. The clip that attaches to the battery pack is a little flimsy and is designed too high for taller people. If you intend to clip the batter pack to your jeans pocket and you're around 6 ft. tall, it probably won't reach and you'll end up clipping it to your shirt. They come with 3 sets of rubber tips in various sizes. I did not find the tips to be as functional as foam tips and the rubber tips would often pop out of my ears while I walked around. So I replaced them with some JVC foam tips I had lying around and they do a much better job at sealing and remaining in place.

In comparing these to the Bose, they don't do the same job. The Bose are superior in every respect of noise canceling. The Sony NC33 earbuds were listed at the store for around $60 and with a 40% discount, I got them for around $36. I checked online and they retail for around $100, so I feel that I got them for a steal! You really can't go wrong with these ear buds, especially if your top priority is to reduce noise and remain portable. I would say that these are ideal for commuting. Though not as good in noise canceling as Bose or other circumaural noise canceling headphones. The NC33 are more than adequate for the job. The the portability aspect and the killer price make these a winner for me!

My scores on a scale of 5 (with 5 being the best):
Sound: 3
Comfort: 4
Quality: 4
Noise canceling: 4
Value: 5+ (if you got them under $50)

Overall, weighing heavily for the functionality of noise canceling and value, I'd give them a 4.5. Thanks Sony!

Brand: Skullcandy Aspects: battery, build, comfort, connectivity, microphone, price, sound Rating: 4.0 Importance Score: 14.54 Review: First off the fit and comfort is better than I was expecting based on the pictures Got these for my kid as a present and they received a warm welcome. For one the Skull Candy has big brand recognition among the tween and teen demographics.

My review is based on some of his feedback and me spending a few days playing with them and using all of the features.
First off the fit and comfort is better than I was expecting based on the pictures. The part that wraps around your neck is very noticeable at first but gets far less noticeable over time. However this can be removed altogether to make it even lighter. With just the minimal wires and a clip to keep everything secure behind your neck. In general I preferred the wire only option but neither was a problem.

The set comes with two sets of ear socks to help customize the fit of the ear bud in your ear canal. I found the smaller sixe to be most comfortable which was a bit of a surprise as I expected an adult to need the bigger ones. They are also shaped differently to better fit the ear canal. One is more rounded with the other set is more of a bullet shape. Either way they stayed in place and were comfortable enough. Far better feeling than standard Apple ear buds. As with all ear buds you’ll occasionally need to adjust them to keep them in place but again seemed less than with the standard Apple buds.

Charging is simple and fast with the included USB cable (aside from the USB and the ear socks nothing else is included). They arrived with some chare so an additional half hour was all it took to bring them up to full. I never stress tested the battery to see how long it lasts under continuous use. But I never ran out of battery. I just threw them on the charger every night and they were good to go the rest of the day. But your mileage will vary here as I don’t use them as much as my kid will.

Sound quality was very good overall however, like with many headphones in this price category they are very bass heavy. Heavy bass is generally a cost effective way to make casual listeners think the sound quality is better than it really is. But in this case the bass is still nice and tight and doesn’t create any buzz or distortion even at higher volumes. But you clearly lose out on the high notes and the mid-range gets a bit muddled. But most people using this headphone daily won’t notice that subtlety and will appreciate the bass.

Connection via Bluetooth to the phone was as easy as any other BT device you’ve used. Once connected you can use them for phone calls and of course to listen to music as you would with the Apple buds with inline volume buttons.

Overall these represent a cost effective way to get good quality wireless ear buds. Most other decent bubs of this quality are priced 5 to 20 dollars higher. I am pleased with the value here and unless you demand precise sound reproduction you’ll be happy with these.

Worth the money so far!

Update:
3 months in with these headphones and I am absolutely in love. The sound has improved with time - much warmer than the first few weeks.

I have been on a number of flights with these headphones now and the noise cancellation optimizer is really impressive. I just open up the air vent all the way and run the optimizer and the whole plane disappears.

The ambient noise - voice feature is not as powerful as I would like but I do really love the headphones. They are comfortable for long periods of time and are really a slick design so I don't feel like a nerd wearing them in professional settings.

I also have to admit that the "palm to quiet" feature is excellent but I do not use it as regularly as I would have hoped. There is still some stigma associated with having big cans on while having a conversation so I normally just remove them if someone comes up to me to discuss anything.

The battery life is insane - I don't even think about it. I only charge them every few days.

I think all the 5-star reviews really dilute the real value of giving something the absolute pinnacle rating - but these deserve it.

The best thing I can say about these headphones is that if I lost them I would immediately buy another pair and never look back.

Brand: Sennheiser Aspects: anc, battery, comfort, connectivity, microphone, price, sound Rating: 5.0 Importance Score: 74.26 Review: Superb - a Non-audiophiles Review I have to confess I am not an audiophile but Sennheiser is starting to change that. A year ago I got [[ASIN:B002YOENAS Sennheiser PXC 310 BT Compact Noise-Canceling Travel Headphones with Bluetooth Technology (Black)]] and I have fallen in love with them. When I got a chance to get a review pair for MM 450's I jumped. These are ever bit as good as old ones and I can't wait to use them on a flight soon.

--Use: It is very easy to pair these to blue tooth devices. I mostly use these in conjunction with an iPod Touch. In the evening I usually switch from speakers to headphones so as not to annoy the neighbors and the headphones take over immediately when I turn them on. Also I enjoy being able to skip forward and backward in the playlists by tapping the buttons on the ear piece. I still have issues with missed phone calls, but I get absolutely no distractions when working with them on (short of a fire alarm.)

--Sound: The Sennheiser sound is better than any headphones I have ever had. I'm not a big fan of base and at first I felt that they did not get loud enough, but now I appreciate that I cranked my headphones to get over room noise. With the noise canceling this is not an issue.

--Design: I love the folding design. they fold flat and fairly narrow and fit into its well designed carrying case well. The smaller ear muffs are not as hot as the full sized ones which is nice and fit comfortably on the head, although sometimes I feel a little twist due to the folding hinges. But all in all they are well crafted and comfortable.

Pros
--Great Sound
--Great Remote controls for my iPod
--A usable and classy carrying case for cables/headphones
--Extreme ease of use
--Hard-wired cable works great
--Long lasting batter - rarely need to recharge
--Amazing folding design makes it fit into my computer bag very well

Cons
--I find little point to the talk through option
--Sometimes noise canceling cuts you off from the world a little too much
--Hard-wired cable seems proprietary so I'm afraid to lose it
--Makes it hard to live with cheaper headphones

I'm still not an audiophile, but Sennheiser has made it impossible for me to go back to my old cheap headphones. You do get what you pay for, and these are worth the price.

Brand: Sony Aspects: anc, battery, build, comfort, connectivity, microphone, price, sound Rating: 4.0 Importance Score: 91.67 Review: A good set of wireless NC earbuds, but somewhat large and pricey In addition to being a headphone addict, I was looking for another option for a quality set of wireless NC ear buds for air travel. Currently I use over-the-ear Sony WH1000XM (original V1) OR Bose Wired in ear QC20's. Both sound good and have very good noise cancelling. However, I wanted to reduce the bulk while traveling and have an option to go wireless as my new phone does not have an audio jack. Enter this review, and my comparison between the Sony WF 1000XM4's and the Google Pixel Buds Pro (PBP).

In this review, I used a 0- or 1-point system with 1 point for the winner, 0 points for the loser and 1 point for each for a tie.

If you don't want to read through the comparison, just get the PBP's. These two earbuds are very close in features, sound quality and noise cancelling, but the PBP's are cheaper and still beat out the Sony's based on my scoring...and I really love the Sony’s but cannot justify them for the price difference!

For my smaller ears the Sony's were a bit more uncomfortable. Ultimately both are really good ear buds, and it comes down to a matter of fit and price (at least for me). If price is not an option and you have larger ears go with Sony, otherwise go with the Pixel Buds Pro.

Detailed review
Price: Both were purchased on sale, so the Sony's were $250 (regularly $280) and the PBP's were $150 (regularly $199). In my opinion, PBP's are a bargain compared to the Sony's! Winner: PBP

Fit: PBP smaller and lighter. Sony, foam tips seal well, but can be scratchy in the ear and feel hotter. For my smaller ears the PBP's were just more comfortable. Winner: PBP

Noise Cancelling: Sony is better with NC and foam tips. PBP's not far behind though. With proper ear tips, the Sony's will block out slightly more noise in air travel. In the office, it is also close, but some of the higher pitched sounds will make it through on the PBP's. Overall, it is pretty close given the cost difference between the two, and Sony's reputation for Noise cancelling. Winner: Sony

Bass -no/flat EQ: Both are bass heavy buds. Winner: Tie

Mid - no/flat EQ: Both have OK Mids. Sony's seem slightly more subdued, but both were pretty muddy without some EQ help. The PBP's treble boost may also help the MIDs come out a little more but its too close to call. Winner: Tie

High - no/flat EQ: PBP have an accentuated high-range making music a little brighter but can get shrill. The Sony's highs were a little dull making for an overall slightly subdued sound Winner: PBP

Overall sound no/flat EQ: Sony's sound slightly cleaner out of the box without the EQ, but the two are close. They both need some EQ help to go from good to great sounding buds. Winner: Sony

Overall Sound W/EQ: With some EQ adjustments both can be closely matched. Sony needs a treble bump, PBP's need treble reduced. Both need the mid’s bumped slightly. Winner: Tie

Touch Controls: PBP has more controls available at once. Both can be changed in the apps, but with Sony you must choose between music control or volume control. The PBP's can do both with taps or swipes. Both will pause music when the earbuds are removed. Winner: PBP

Control feedback: Sony's have spoken audio queue's, PBP's use beeps. Much easier to know what you changed with spoken responses by the Sony's. Winner: Sony

Volume level: At the same volume settings, the Sony's were louder. For the PBP's it seemed I needed to play these near max volume. Could be a phone issue, but all volume and app settings were the same for both. This bothered me enough to almost make me not want to keep the PBP's. Winner: Sony

Battery Life (ANC on): Sony's seem to have a slight edge, but only by a few minutes. Too close to call Winner: Tie

Charging: Both have USB C and Wireless charging and charge in approximately the same amount of time. Number of charges and quick charging (5-minute charge for an hour of play) is the same per the specs. I have not tried this out yet as I always keep these charged. Winner: Tie

Multipoint (pairing): Sony does not allow you to pair and use with two Bluetooth devices at the same time. The PBP's do. Shame on Sony for not including this for the price of these headphones. Great feature for office use! Winner: PBP

App: Both apps are well done. The PBP app is a little easier to use. Both have ear tip check feature which will tell you if you ear tips are sealing well. I used a smaller tip in one ear and both apps detected a bad seal. Winner: Tie

Charging Case size: Both are small, but the PBP's have slimmer case and feels better when carried in the pocket. Winner: PBP

Assistant features: Both are capable of using Google assistant or Alexa..Yes, the PBPs will work with Alexa Winner: Tie

Auto Ambient Switch: Only available on the Sony. If it senses you are talking it will automatically switch to ambient sound pass through and pause your music. Very useful feature and works well especially on an airplane. However, if you like to sing out loud you probably want to disable this feature. Winner: Sony

Find my headphones: Only available on the PBPs. You can send a signal and make the buds beep if you misplace them. Winner: PBP

Bluetooth: Both connect instantly and have great range. Walking to the other side of my house and through about 3 walls, both ear buds cut out in the exact same location. Winner: Tie

Microphone: Did not really test this, based on the soundguys.com review they are close, but in their recorded audio the PBP's mic sounds a bit better to my ear than the Sony's. Winner: PBP

Total Score: Sony 13, Pixel Buds Pro 16

~~~Original Review~~~

Okay...first and foremost, these headphones are my first pair of wireless headphones. Second, these are also my first headphones with active noise cancelling technology. I have used these for 2 months now. This review is for the Sony MDR-100ABN/L h.ear on Wireless NC- Blue. I purchased these at BestBuy for approximately $180 on sale.

~~~~~SOUND~~~~~

These headphones are of excellent quality overall. The audio quality is exceptional. Highs are high and lows are low. Bass is heavy and gives a nice thump. Overall, sound is very crisp. The sound stage is very wide, allowing for a surround sound experience. These headphones are closed back therefore there is very little to no sound leak even at higher volumes.

~~~~~COMFORT~~~~~

These fit fairly well on the head although some may find them a bit too tight. Like most headphones, this should loosen up in time. The earpieces swivel about 45 degrees for adjustable comfort. The headband adjusts approximately 2 inches on both sides to allow for a variety of head sizes and shapes. The ear cups are made of a soft faux leather material with much cushion. When wearing these headphones, the ear pieces fit around your ears. Your entire ear fits inside the ear piece. Overall, I found these to be quite comfortable with proper adjustment of the headband height.

~~~~~BATTERY LIFE~~~~~

I have tested how long the battery lasts on these headphones. I wrote down each and every time I turn on the device and turn off the device. With general usage on the computer, I will get anywhere from 22 hours to 28 hours of usage. Do note that this is not CONSTANT usage in terms of audio playback. This is the amount of time that the headphones are connected via Bluetooth to the computer. I estimate about 16 to 18 hours of the time above is constant playback. Overall, the battery life is very exceptional in my opinion. It takes about 5 hours to charge the battery via a USB connection on a computer. The manufacturer (Sony) recommends that you only charge this device using a USB port on a computer. I have not tried charging via USB power brick (i.e., smart phone plug-in USB to outlet). The charging cable that is included with these headphones is very short at approximately 2 feet long. These headphones charge via Micro USB. Overall, I have to charge the headphones maybe once every 3 days depending on usage. I will test these in the future with constant playback to see how long the battery lasts.

~~~~~FEATURES~~~~~

I found several features to be quite nice with these headphones. The active noise cancelling is especially nice. When you turn on the device, it will automatically turn on the active noise cancelling as well. You can turn this off at any time while connected wirelessly via the appropriate button above the power button on the left side earpiece. The active noise cancelling eliminates constant noises such as computer fans, vehicle traffic noise, and human conversations. Though do note that you will still hear talking but it is greatly reduced in volume. I assume this is so that you can still hear someone talking to you for safety reasons or otherwise.

There is also a volume rocker button on the right earpiece. This is nice to have for quick access to lower or raise the volume of the audio at any point in time. Above the volume rocker on the same side is a slider/button which gives you playlist features such as pause, play, previous, and next.

This set of headphones also features a removable headphone jack. The cable provided is thick and the outer insulation has a premium rubbery feel to it. The cable is the same matching color as the headphones themselves. You can plug the cable directly into the headphones. Doing so does not use any battery capacity. Though, I found the cable to be quite short overall. You may want to invest in a longer cable is you wish to use these headphones wired to a computer. Though, it may be a decent length for smartphone use.

These headphones also feature dual microphones. You can use these headphones as a wireless headset for calls on a smartphone or via a computer. The microphones are also used for the active noise cancelling. If you blow air into the microphones while in use, you will hear it. I do not recommend these for outdoor use as wind would be an issue unless you are not using the active noise cancelling feature while outdoors.

~~~~~STORAGE~~~~~

These headphones feature a collapsible design in which the earpieces fold into the headband for compact storage. These headphones also come with a nice hard storage case of matching color. The texture of the case is of cloth material. The case opens and closes via zipper. Also worth noting is that the case allows for just enough room for the charging cable and headphone jack cable. The case is hard enough to protect the headphones if the case is dropped. The inside of the case is a soft material.

~~~~~DURABILITY~~~~~

I have had these headphones for around 2 months now and they still look brand new. The outer shell of the headphones are made of a metallic material similar to aluminum. Brand: Audio-Technica Aspects: build, comfort, connectivity, price, sound Rating: 5.0 Importance Score: 16.10 Review: You've found what you were looking for Let me start by saying I own several pairs of $150+ headphones, including the VMODA M-100 and Seinnheiser HD 598. And, in my opinion, the ATH-M50x is by far the best pair of headphones you could possibly buy in this price range. If you're considering these headphones and are on the fence, I strongly encourage you to make the jump - you won't be disappointed. If, however, you need a little more convincing, I'll break down why I love these cans so much.

First and foremost, the sound quality is absolutely unreal. What can I say that hasn't already been said? These have a deep, rich sound. They're not overly heavy on the bass - it's just right. They provide a wonderfully accurate frequency response and make any genre of music sound great. Rock, pop, jazz, classical, folk, country...regardless of what you're into, these will fit the bill.

In my eyes, comfort is just as important as sound quality. If you can't wear a pair of headphones for long periods, it doesn't matter how good they sound. Fortunately, these are supremely comfortable. The faux-leather ear pads / headband are very soft, and the padding is plentiful. I can easily wear them for 4+ hours straight without any discomfort on the sides of my head and ears. They are a closed-back, over-ear model...so if you plan on wearing them outside they will get a little toasty on a hot day, but that comes with the territory on this type of model. Honestly, I can't imagine a more comfortable pair of headphones.

The build quality is very solid. If you're used to VMODA's tank-like structural design, the plastic on the M50x might feel a little weak at first, but you'll quickly realize there's no need to baby them. The swivel hinges are well made, the headband is solid metal, and the overall feel of the headphone inspires confidence that you can throw them in the back of your car without worry.

Last but not least - the price. You simply won't find a better sounding, more comfortable pair of headphones unless you're willing to spend a lot more money. The value offered by the ATH-M50x cannot be matched by any pair of cans in this price range, period. It really is that simple.

Review: Extreme value for the lowered $50 price do These were originally about $120-130 and I got these for $50 at Best Buy. Amazon sells them for this price now, too. These are awesome. To begin, when you put them on, you'll realize they have pretty decent sound isolation for a non-anc pair of headphones. This was the first thing I noticed when trying them on in store and a strong plus. The sound is great as well. Boosted bass but not obnoxiously overwhelming and the mids and highs are decently balanced. I'd say the worst sound feature is that they are slightly muddy if you really pay attention or are an audiophile. I listen to hours of music everyday and these are still high quality cans. To compare, I have a pair of Sony wh1000mx3 ($300+ and one of the best cans on the market) and if those are 8.5/10 in sound, these are about a 6.5/10. For $50 compared to $300+ and only minimal difference in sound is phenomenal. I use these only for the gym because I don't want to ruin my Sony ones. They are made cheaper obviously but it's never something I really notice nor care about. I've had them for months and I'm honestly not the most careful person and I haven't experienced any cracks or defects like others have shared.  Battery life is strong to this day. Connectivity is easy and I never have issues. The only gripe that would've rendered these perfect would be that the buttons for volume and turning on(the only buttons) are not the best (I'm being very nitpicky) and there are no additional features like Google Assistant. Really for $50 that'd be asking for a lot but those are the only things I can fault. When these were $130, I'd give them a high 3 or a low 4 out of 5 but for $50 I can't imagine a better Bluetooth over ear headphone that isn't 4-6x more expensive.

Brand: Audio-Technica Aspects: anc, connectivity, price, sound Rating: 5.0 Importance Score: 104.89 Review: Only a turn table, no speakers When you're unfamiliar with record players, what they need or consist of, asking yourself questions as "what is Resonance?" or just overwhelmed with all these new parts such as needles, tone arms, turntable mat's, and such.
Well, I can tell you right now, if you want to enjoy you're records and music sessions for yourself, you're going to want to go with Audio Technica. I believe this may be one of their cheaper models, but I don't think you need to over extend you're wallet if you're unfamiliar with all of the choices out there. So why this one? Easy.

it's a freakin' AWESOME record player! The record starts spinning at a press of a button, with a tone arm to follow in suit after. Simply place you're record of you're audio-technica turntable mat, and press the button. (After assembly) Don't feel like pressing the button and want to drop the needle yourself? go right ahead; the response time is immediate. The ATXLP60 comes in 3 color options; I chose orange because ... why not.
This is probably the best record player for a newbie, or for someone who wants to give a great gift in a new hobby.

Assembly: I've never had to "assemble" a record player, but it really gave me a sweet satisfaction seeing it put together, and understand some of the mechanics of the process. The instructions, obviously, work. I wouldn't stress about breaking anything; it's mostly setting a plate on the turn table, and making sure the belt is wrapped around the left side spindle. The record player is wide, but light weight. After using a large Crossly for so long, I'm very happy with the size of the product.

Giftability: Yes! But be sure to have a good pair of speakers to come along, otherwise, you're friend isn't going to be able to listen to their records. Packaging for the products are legit, and just arrive in pristine condition.

Overall: Normally you can get all in 1 record player for this price, with all the gizmo's and attachments. With the speakers not built into the record player, we are looking at no resonance feedback.As long as you take care of the record player, it's going to last longer than the needle. Which, mind you, needs replacing after awhile, in case you were not aware. Audio-Technica also specializes in needles, which is great perk. I'd like to mention that the needles for these "all in one" record players have a cheaper model of needle, and may damage your records in long term use. I skipped out on the blue tooth option, which i slightly regret now after buying a good pair of speakers, but I feel that it's a non essential in record player needs.

Brand: Sony Aspects: anc, battery, build, comfort, microphone, sound Rating: 5.0 Importance Score: 13.00 Review: Tons of options to control these AND can lay down with them!!! I had beats before this, and they hurt my ears and my head because they were so weirdly shaped and tight. Like my ears would literally fall asleep. I seriously don't even notice I'm wearing them. No tightness, no pressure, no ears falling asleep.

Brand: Sony Aspects: anc, battery, comfort, sound Rating: 4.0 Importance Score: 5.69 Review: Above average offering, but don't care for the design of them or the gesture controls This product feels different to me than other Sony stuff I've gotten. It's like they've gone out of their way to make everything feel like cardboard. The packaging was like egg carton, and the earbuds and their charging station are a rough lightweight material in a drab color. The earbuds themselves are customizable, but bulky compared to the competition. I find them hard to get out of the container, hard to hold onto, and hard to wear. The noise cancellation and sound are both above average though. I like the charging light on the container. It communicates with color and flashes what the status of the product is, and isn't bright or hard to locate.

USB-C charging makes the base station charge up nice and fast. It holds the earbuds securely, and doesn't rattle or fall open. It's also a nice pocketable size and shape with no sharp corners, making the whole product especially portable. Controls are gesture, rather than button. I'm not a fan of this because I end up doing a lot of swiping and hoping. A click button interface, I can just click it the number of times I know will do what I need, and move on.

I've got a few Sony audio products that I use every day and am very happy with, but with earbuds, I'm sticking with jabra for now. It's a competitive market right now for earbuds. Lots of companies putting out new designs.

Brand: Audio-Technica Aspects: comfort, price, sound Rating: 4.0 Importance Score: 8.32 Review: Excellent sound. Okay comfort I had these in my cart for a while and just recently made the purchase. I've owned all of your sub $500 headphones Beyer 770, 880, 990, HD 560s, 6xx, Meze Neo etc. The ATH-M50X is way better than I though it would be. I had been using my HD 560s' for the last few months. I mainly use my phones for work and gaming. These fit the bill for both. Sound stage and imaging is great on these. Clarity and bass is perfect. The only downside is out of all of my headphones these have 2 big issues. That is why I gave it 4 stars instead of 5.

2. Heat. Again, more than any of my other headphones these produce some crazy heat. In a comfortable room temperature house, your ears will sweat after a couple of hours. The heat obviously radiates and it can make you hot overall.

For the price these are hard to beat. If I change the pads I'll update the review. If these had more comfortable pads these would easily be a 10/10. I highly recommend.

Update 3: For those who put the iPhone in their front pocket and loose connectivity understand that the wifi/Bluetooth antenna is located at the top of the back if the phone. So when I place it in my front pocket I point the screen towards my leg with the phone in the up position to protect it from bumps but it also places the wifi/Bluetooth antenna up and out. By doing this I never loose a signal.

Update 2: Well I've had a few days to evaluate these and I can find no fault. They sound incredible but like all earphones you have to find the ear piece that fits your ear best. I've settled on the large ear pieces and the work great but you still have to move them around your ear a bit to get the best bass response.

I ordered the Bose charging case and it's sooo much better and larger than the cheap smallish case you get with the earphones but is it worth $49.00...not in my opinion but it is much better than stock and it keeps your SoundSport charged.

Review: Maintaining my vows of silence Audio-Technica ATH-50x's have been my go to headphones for the last two years and they have held up admirably.  I have owned some well respected phones over the last 15 years so I take buying a set seriously and feel finding any that sound better at the same price levels as these would be difficult if not impossible.  I am no audiophile but feel the sound quality to be excellent. There are numerous reviews by audiophiles that have much more nuanced detail of the sound characteristics of these headphones.  In my case if they have a limitation it is with comfort. I have a large head and using them several hours a day has lead to sore ears.  People with smaller head sizes or who use them less would probably be fine.  Cautious tip here:if you find them too tight you can gently pull the cups outward taking some of the tension off the head band and they won't squeeze your ears as tightly.  Once bent I couldn't go back so I was careful here doing it slowly in stages until I hit as much of an optimum as I dared resulting in a much improved comfort level. I recently purchased a set of significantly more comfortable and better sounding headphones but they were also significantly more expensive as well [[ASIN:B000F2BLTM Beyerdynamic DT 880 Premium 250 ohm HiFi headphones]]  The catch beside the price is that the Beyerdynamics are open back headphones which improves the sound quality but also insures people standing nearby will hear your music.  I don't like being forced to listen to other people's music so I can hardly force them to listen to mine which means the Audio-Technica ATH-M50x's are still my go to cans for using mass transit or any other crowded environments. The AT's also isolate me from ambient sound better than the Beyerdynamics which can be useful in noisy situations. The AT's have low impedance and play very well with my various mobile devices and are capable of delivering volume well into the painful levels. With the possible exception of the lower end Grado headphones like the [[ASIN:B00KYTNU9U Grado SR60e Headphones]] which are highly regarded for sound quality but not so much for comfort and are also open backed and thus intrusive to bystanders,the ATH's may be the best bang for the buck of headphones that I know of. I know of no closed back phones in the ATH's price range that sound as good.

Brand: Audio-Technica Aspects: anc, battery, build, comfort, connectivity, price, sound Rating: 5.0 Importance Score: 6.21 Review: These things ROCK! -Updated 9/12/21 I plan to start recording music of my own at home. So, I've been researching everything I'd need. A decent pair of monitor headphones are at the top of the "essential" list. I figured that if I were going to be spending money on some headphones, I'd like them to work casual use as well as mixing. So, after lots of digging, reading reviews, watching comparison videos, I have determined that the M50x would be the best for me, for the following reasons:

* Excellent sound quality
* Relatively flat response rate
* Comfortable with glasses
* Detachable cables (included with purchase)
* Reasonably affordable

I'm glad I put in all the legwork ahead of time. These arrived yesterday, and I've worn them for several hours, listening to music (mostly rock, metal and pop) and playing games (Diablo 3 and Skyrim.) The sound quality is outstanding. Admittedly, these are the first studio monitors I've listened to. I've owned decent "fun" headphones in the past (~$50 range) and these are by far superior to what I've experienced.

It took me a bit to settle on these, as some folks claimed that they were either bass heavy, harsh, or uncomfortable. Fortunately, I've found none of this to be true. I've had these on for up to 2 hours with no discomfort what-so-ever. When it comes to the sound, all I can say is "wow!" Everything is extremely clear, and I feel, balanced. The bass is definitely there, but it's not overpowering, and it certainly doesn't detract from other frequencies. The high end comes in really well, and not harsh at all to me. The layers in the music I've listened to so far are so much easier to distinguish.

In the past, I've been able to accurately distinguish various bitrate MP3s/audio files over laptop speakers (128 vs 256 vs lossless.) These headphones make it way easier.

Update - I've recently tried 2 other sets of headphones that my be somewhat comparable: Rockville Pro M50's and the Sony WHCH710N. The M50x's are by far better than both. The Rockville headphones are 1/3 the price and actually pretty close, if you take the price into comparison. The Audio Technicas are a fair amount clearer with nicer highs and lows. The Sony headphones were fairly disappointing, and I wouldn't recommend them, unless you needed wireless. I'm still very happy with my purchase.

Brand: Anker Aspects: anc, battery, build, comfort, connectivity, microphone, price, sound Rating: 5.0 Importance Score: 42.20 Review: wow, great sound, incredible value I have become obsessed with finding the best general purpose bluetooth headphones and have (unfortunately) purchased and evaluated several pairs. I have not been satisfied with most of them. These Anker SoundBuds are pretty spectacular, especially considering how affordable they are. Here are my rating criteria and the results (tests performed on iPhone 6S Plus running iOS 11.2.6):
1. Pairing: A+
Pairing could not have been any easier. Worked the first time, done in seconds.
2. Audio Fidelity: B+
These headphones lean toward bass. I would prefer a flatter frequency curve so that I can get the sound I want through equalizer, but a lot of people will probably appreciate the heavier bass response in these. This makes them a little better for music, video, gaming than for spoken word (podcast / phone call).
3. Loudness: B-
These get loud, but I would prefer it if they could get just a little louder. Still, the audio fidelity at maximum loudness is outstanding, there is no noticeable degradation in quality like what happens on some headphones.
4. Latency/Lag: A
Bluetooth is inherently laggy, so I've learned not to expect perfection. I'm practically in shock at how little lag there is. There is still some, but it is negligible... making these headphones suitable for both video and gaming in addition to music. So far, these are the best bluetooth headphones I have ever tried with regard to signal latency.
5. Comfort/Fit: A+
They come with 4 sets of tips and 4 sets of 'wings' (small, medium, large, extra large). They came pre-installed with the large size, which fits me perfectly. They are really comfortable and feel very secure in place.
6. Passive noise blocking: A
These do well at blocking out a lot of external noise. Not quite as good as what you'd get from a set of Etymotic buds, but close enough to be competitive.
7. Controls: A+
I think controls should be obvious and make sense without having to read a manual... and these are. They are simple, intuitive, easy to use, and big enough to manage with gloves on.
8. Charging & Battery:
I don't yet have enough data to give a rating on charge/battery performance, but I'll update this review in a few months after I've broken the batter in by using them and charging them daily.
9. Value: A+
Summary: these are currently the best bluetooth headphones I have, at any price.

Brand: Sony Aspects: anc, battery, build, comfort, connectivity, microphone, sound Rating: 5.0 Importance Score: 5.65 Review: Sony vs. Airpods I'm not an audiophile, but I was looking for great sound, noise cancellation, and fit. Brand: Sony Aspects: anc, comfort, connectivity, microphone, sound Rating: 4.0 Importance Score: 83.74 Review: Very Solid For Out-of-Office I left a lengthy review of the Jabra Evolve2 85 (E285) vs Bose Quiet Comfort 35 II (QC35II) vs Bose Noise Cancelling 700 (B700) vs Sony WH-1000XM4 (SX4) (in order of when I received them) for the E285. Since that review is out there, I'll reduce this one only to E285 and SX4.

Microphone and Playback
The E285 was notably better than the SX4, as evidenced by my recordings while drumming. It’s neck and neck as far as clarity of voice goes.

Akin to the E285, the SX4 are quite good as far as pick-up goes. But unfortunately, they were a little too good (or simply worse at distinguishing background noise). A co-worker and I had a Zoom meeting with ourselves in the same closet (to maximize productivity), switching between the headsets. Whoever had the E285 could hear themselves through the headset. At first, I thought it was because of the function where you hear yourself through the headset. But alas, upon one of us leaving the room, the echo went away. This meant the SX4 was picking up the other’s voice! The main killer of the SX4 is that you have no way to mute yourself from the headset. As a pair of headphones to be used in the office, this is crucial. You never know when someone may decide to butt in your closet (or office, for most people) and shout “HHHHEEEYYYY!!”. In contrast, the E285 offers two ways to protect yourself from such intrusions – by raising the boom arm or by pressing the button on the boom arm, leaving you to look down simply in resignation at said co-worker without having disrupted the meeting. The one drawback with the E285 is that sometimes the first word or two don’t quite make it through, so you may develop a stutter of your introduction. “I’m Jason – oh – I’M JASON – oh you can hear me now? I’m Jason”. Good news is, you shan’t be soon forgotten. Despite this, it’s more favorable than not being able to mute myself, though I can understand disagreement with this point. Win for E285. Runner up is SX4.

Audio Output - DISCLAIMER - I'm not an audiophile
Both associated apps come with decent equalizers (unlike Bose). Honestly, it was difficult to tell the difference between the E285 and SX4. I think the amount of bass you get is comparable, however I think the SX4 is capable of producing an ever-so-slightly louder sound. Win for SX4. Runner up is E285.

Brand
Jabra: doesn’t require my location. Instead, it lets me know if I desire to give it my location, it will use it to locate my headphones. No, but thank you for giving me an actual choice!

Sony: same as Jabra, though with a caveat. The SX4 offers more utility when given Location permission, which will be addressed later on. They were not pertinent to me, however, so my location remains an enigma for Sony and Jabra (and Bose). Tie between E285 and SX4.

ANC
When playing on a drumpad, the E285 did a noticeably better job than the other headsets. It sounds like the pad is being muffled (which is what I’m looking for), whereas the others don’t quite succeed. The E285 does a better job with impact noises (or maybe it’s just higher frequencies) than the other headsets, though the SX4 is perfectly satisfactory.

In addition, the hear-through function of the E285 is awesome. It almost makes it sound like you’re not wearing them (tested at maximum hear-through). The SX4 has essentially the same feature, with an added “Focus on voice” option. I couldn’t tell a difference when that was on/off. Besides that, the SX4 didn’t do as good a job at allowing sound through as the E285. Win for E285. Runner up is SX4.

Connectivity
The E285 and SX4 have longer ranges than the QC35II. The E285, most of the time, reconnects automatically when coming back into range. Unfortunately, I don't remember specifics about the SX4. SX4 and E285 have a 3.5 mm jack. Why doesn’t the B700? Because it sucks.

Double-connection to my PC (independent of range): E285 is easier because it’s just plug-and-play, no downloads or “connecting”. The QC35II and SX4 are only Bluetooth, so you have to do the standard “add device”, etc. One annoying thing about the SX4 is you have to use the app to establish a Bluetooth connection to another device. Not a big deal, but for comparison’s sake, the E285 is better. An added feature of the Jabra is Jabra Direct, a software you can download to better manage your Jabra. It gives you a few more options and is worth using, in this writer’s humble opinion.

Response time: The SX4 is the fastest, though we’re talking minute (not 60 seconds) differences. The E285 is on the cusp of being slow enough to be annoying, but not quite. The E285 and SX4 have the cool feature of pausing media when the headphones are removed from your skull. Again, the E285 feel lack-luster in comparison because they take approximately 4 M-I-S-S-I-S-S-I-P-I seconds to pause, whereas the SX4 is half that time. For the E285, I’ve noticed the ear detection only works properly when playing music from your phone and not the PC. When using it with the PC, if I remove the headphones, the music will pause as it should. But it doesn’t resume when I put them back on. If I pause the music with the button, then remove the headset, it resumes. Again, cool feature, but needs work, especially when using it with the PC. So SX4 is better about ear detection (presumably because it's laser-assisted).

App connectivity: some issues with E285. Some issues with SX4. LOTS of issues with B700 (Bose Music). Unfortunately, apps are prone to some bugs every now and then. I can’t say which of the two (Sony/Jabra) had more, so neither bothered me much. Winner is SX4 (better media response time). Runner up is E285.

Voice Assistant
The E285 and SX4 worked exactly as expected. No setup or anything, I just pushed the button and my assistant came up. The caveat for the E285 is you have to pull down the boom arm to use the feature (you can still press the action button with the boom arm up and have the assistant prompt, but because the arm is up, the microphone is off so it’s pointless). Tie between E285 and SX4.

Controls
On the E285, the buttons take up little surface area and are rather flat. Muting is done with the E285 by simply raising or lowering the boom mic. In contrast, the SX4 doesn't have any way to mute yourself.

After just a little use of the “touchless” controls (B700 and SX4), I can see their usefulness. It’s certainly easier to play/pause music and use the Voice Assistant (which is no easier to actually setup because Bose sucks). Changing volume is annoying because every click up/down requires an extra swipe. As debilitating as this is, one would not likely be changing by a bunch of increments at a time. After more use of the touchless controls, I much prefer the them over the physical ones. Win for SX4. Runner up is E285.

Comfort and Style
Comfort is pretty much a tie between E285 and SX4. I've worn both for hours and hours without any issue. But I prefer the style of the SX4 - it just feels more premium. Winner is SX4. Runner up is E285.

Extra
The E285 has the hear-through feature, which I really like because I use ANC only when there are sounds I actively don’t want to listen to, like from mine or my roommate’s drumming, running water, laundry, phone call, or pooping with the fan on. Other than those times, I want some awareness of my surroundings because there’s nothing more frustrating than trying to get the attention of someone with headphones on (especially at work). In addition, the E285 and SX4 have ear detection (discussed previously). The E285 has a great way of handling multiple calls with its huge button on the right cuff. You can switch between two phone calls by putting one on hold and accept/end/reject calls using it. This is RARELY used, but it's cool.

Issues
There’s some variability with functionality of the Google Assistant with the E285. At the very least, the action button on the arm activates the assistant. But sometimes the input for said assistant is on the phone rather than the arm. Most of the time it works as expected. I think the additional connection to the PC adds complexity that needs to be vetted out for seamless functionality for the E285.

Final verdict, best to worst: E285, SX4 (killer - no mute function, worse hear-through), QC35II (killers - older BT connection, worse audio, poor ANC). Literally wouldn't buy B700.

UPDATE: It's been several weeks since I returned all but the Jabra Evolve2 85 (I use it 3-10 hours every single day) and my final rating is four stars, same as the SX4. When the E285 works, it's great. But it doesn't work all the time, unfortunately. I can't say the same for the SX4 since it was ultimately returned. I ended up choosing the E285 over the SX4 because it has an edge when it comes to office use, for one main reason - you can't mute yourself on the SX4. But if muting yourself directly from your headset isn't important to you and you're not typically in a noisy environment during calls, then I would honestly recommend the Sony WH-1000XM4.
