Brand: SoundPEATS Aspects: build, comfort, sound Rating: 5.0 Importance Score: 2.42 Review: Great starter phones for sports I received these as a free review sample eight months ago. Since there are a lot of detailed reviews I will focus on my experience with these and also compare them to other sports phones.

First, these are good quality for twenty buck phones. But do be aware that sports phones are designed for comfort more than sound quality. Not that these are marginal - they have a typical bias towards the mid range, with compressed highs and adequate bass. They are not going to win acclaim from audiophiles, but do reproduce music that is clear and perfect for background listening.

Another thing I like is comfort. I can wear these for a few hours at a time without even noticing them. For sports phones that is a major plus. These allow in a little ambient noise for safety, but it does not appreciably affect the quality of music. Volume is somewhat limited. If you like music super loud these are not going to make you happy. On the other hand, high volume defeats the purpose of sports phones since you need to be aware of ambient noise and your surroundings. I cannot emphasize strongly enough that these are NOT general purpose phones.

Finally, the specs claim about four hours of music play time. That is true with the volume at about a third. If you crank the volume to max you can cut that time in half.

Review: Fantastic Find Sleek, compact, easily paired, comfortable, and high quality. This headset easily paired with both of my Bluetooth devices without a problem. Headset is light, sleek, and easy to use. I was amazed at the quality of the sound and how well noise in the room was cancelled out. The only drawback I found was with the position of the buttons and where you place the band, you will need to spend some extra time to accustom yourself to the location of the buttons. This will take practice and until you are familiar with the button locations - you will find yourself taking them off until you have their locations memorized by touch. Doesn't take long. Now that I am familiar with the location of the feature buttons, I feel like a pro with this headset. I would definitely recommend this to someone shopping for wireless headphones as these are a great value, very good quality, and are very comfortable. The drawback of familiarizing yourself to the buttons while wearing the band (which goes around the neck), is far outweighed by the quality and features this handy little gadget provides. Some of the features I enjoy the most are: pressing a button to hear what the charge status of the battery is (charges with a USB connection), pause/play, fast forward, rewind, volume up and down, taking calls on the headset, muting calls, and being able to activate call waiting while on another call. The package comes with extra ear bud cushions and the model itself is available in a handful of other colors to really help reflect your own style. A great buy - so be sure to get one!

The microphone passed a voice to text test with flying colors. In fact, it did so well, I tried an experiment of streaming audio on a speaker several feet away and the transcription from that7 was quite good as well.

Overall, a comfortable Bluetooth headphone with average audio response and really terrific secure fit.

Brand: Bose Aspects: battery, build, comfort, connectivity, microphone, price, sound Rating: 5.0 Importance Score: 4.22 Review: Excellent headphones and worth the price. Actually I’m not sure if these should properly be called headphones, earbuds or something else.

But I have absolutely no doubt that they can be described as excellent.

They have enough bulk for my elderly, somewhat arthritic fingers to securely hold and insert into my ear, hook the “tip wing” under the ear ridge and gently line the earbud (the part that carries the audio) up with your ear canal.

It takes practice to reduce it to an automatic routine. I figure another two or three days before I get the process down pat. Once inserted and secured, the headphones stay in place and are comfortable to wear for hours.

The short cord, in my case, is maybe an inch too short when looped around my neck, but it is a lot better than long cords I’ve had on other units.

The inline control block isn’t comfortably accessible for me. I have limited arm mobility and the control block falls in an inconvenient position for me. I’ll practice using it and maybe can become comfortable with it. If not, the controls on whatever device I’m using will have to do.

Bluetooth pairing is as simple as it gets, though I haven’t tried NFC pairing yet.

The benefit of the Bose “volume-optimized EQ” is difficult to describe. There is definitely less distortion when the volume is cranked up on the iPad Mini I’ve been using it most with. The audio is nice and smooth. I don’t expect bass and I’m not feeling let down by its absence. I’m using these for both music and spoken word and the output is just plain fine. Not superb, but more than adequate.

The inline microphone provides excellent voice quality with Skype and video recording and conferencing.

Bose says the unit takes a full charge in two hours and that seems about rate. Bose also says the phones are good for six hours on a charge. I haven’t run it that much in a day yet, but it sounds short. However, Bose says a 15 minute charge gives you an hour of listening time, so carrying a small external power source sounds like a good remedy.

Bose includes a nice little round case and a sturdy carabiner clip. This is an incredibly wonderful feature.

Overall, ease of use is high, sound quality is more than adequate. Build quality is good.

One word summation: excellent and worth the price.

Jerry

Brand: Audio-Technica Aspects: anc, build, comfort, connectivity, microphone, price, sound Rating: 5.0 Importance Score: 8.37 Review: Excellent headphones for those who know what they're looking for It's "almost" (see my edit below) impossible to find a negative review of the Audio-Technica ATH-M50x Closed Back Headphones. And I have to agree. Probably the best headphones I've ever owned. They've been available now for 10 or so years. You can't get this high end quality audio anywhere else for this price point. I got an excellent deal. The headphone purchase also included 3 high quality extra items for just about the same price as the headphones alone. My package included an extra cable, a solid hard plastic carrying case, and a FiiO A1 headphone amplifier.
The headphones live up to all the positive reviews, and the extras included made for a very good total price.
I would definitely recommend this product.
Updated 2/4/2021, 4 months later.
I still love these headphones. I have to laugh when I occasionally check the customer reviews and find that most of the criticisms are based on how these headphones were purposely engineered. Let me be more specific. Some of the negative comments include:
-others around you can hear what you're listening to. Oh well. Too bad. They should probably put on their own noise cancelling headphones. Which brings me to my next point:
-they're not noise cancelling. Yup, that's right. When I did my search for headphones, I specifically did NOT want noise cancelling OR Bluetooth. Much prefer putting the money saved when not buying these options and putting it toward better cans.
-there is no microphone on the cables. Correct again. These headphones were not necessarily intended for those who want a set of cans primarily to support their cell phone addiction.
-they must be used with the company's proprietary cables to attach to the headphones. I'll take that and love it. Why? Because they twist and lock into the bottom of the can. How many times, with other headphones, have I moved too far, or stood up or whatever, and the cord would be ripped out of the cans!!
-too much bass, not enough base. Midrange too pronounced, or not enough. And they go on and on and on.
-the "best" (tongue in cheek) criticism stated that none of the included cables, nor the included adapters fit his computer. I don't know what kind of a computer this person has, but, if Bill Cosby appears in TV commercials for your computer, headphones should not be your #1 focus right now
-and finally, and I quote, "I needed to add an EQ to make them sound like I wanted them to". Precisely! These cans were designed and manufactured specifically for those who are looking for an accurate, unadulterated representation of the original recording. The way the musicians and the recording engineers intended it to sound. If one doesn't find that acceptable, and that's fine, include an EQ somewhere in the mix. As a matter of fact, I have more than my share of sound shaping hardware and software all over my system in order to tailor the music to my liking.
You need to like the sound quality reproduced through headphones and speaker systems alike. And this is very subjective. One person's likes are another person's hates, especially when it comes from those who love to sound like they "might" know what they are talking about.. I bought and returned a number of headphones because I didn't care for how they sounded. I kept these.
I do agree with one common complaint. The earpads do get uncomfortable after a while. I checked for aftermarket replacements, but many included their own negative reviews that they changed the sound quality from the originals. That's the last thing I want.

Brand: Sony Aspects: anc, battery, build, comfort, connectivity, microphone, price, sound Rating: 5.0 Importance Score: 45.18 Review: Great Sound - 1st Class Noise Cancelling First a side note. Do NOT order this item for overnight delivery. It is a password protected delivery which means that someone will be at your door at 4:00 AM asking for the magic incantation.

I have the comparable Sony earbuds so I knew what to expect. Others will have to contend with micro-printed docs on 5 lb paper and a weird wordless diagram which requires a translation from pantomime to one's native language. HOWEVER, the Sony website has ample documentation in a readable format. Also, you need the Sony headphone app to fine tune the setup.

I have acute PTSD, a consequence of gun violence. My building is undergoing major construction in th4e form of concrete restoration. I suspect that, for most people, the noise from the machines is a mere inconvenience. For me that is not the case. It's a pile driver inside my head.

My earbuds were doing the job (these products have the best noise-cancelling of any device I have tried). However, they have limited battery life. The headphones solve that problem. I CANNOT HEAR A SOUND FROM THE CONSTRUCTION. Moreover, I have modest hearing loss in one ear. Hearing aids balance things out and the headphone can be worn over the aids.

I have a large music collection (mostly jazz and classical) ripped losslessly (flac) to large USB drives. The drives are attached to an Nvidia Shield Pro which is an Android TV device. Bluetooth paired right up, has decent range (not requiring LOS) and connects via LDAC (which is what you want for the best quality). I then use Kodi for playback of music and movies.

On the left side is an on-off button and another button that can be programmed via the app. On the right, the side of the speaker is actually a mouse pad which allows you to control both volume and your player (Kodi is imperfect in that regard but it works fine with the app on my phone).

The music is flat AS IT SHOULD BE. Competing devices seem to be made for the average 16-year-old, artificially pumping up the bass. If unnatural noise is what you want, there are plenty of IOS and Android equalizer apps. In any event Mr. Mozart sounds like he should. Most jazz includes a bass that comes through exactly as it would in a live performance. Well, not exactly because we experience bass physically as well as through hearing.

One final note. These things are big. I have an average-sized head (something my former employees would disagree with). The earpieces are not extended at all and the thing is a bit bigger than my headspace. Also, I wish that they were just a tad lighter.

So, in the final analysis, these offer excellent sound reproduction that is accurate, rich and natural. The controls (which include a mouse pad on the right speaker) allow you to control your device and volume. The noise cancellation could not be better. There are no compromises making this a reasonable value.

I ordered these headphones directly from Audio Technica after seeing their announcement. My favorite pair of headphones is their ATH-M40x, and I was curious how their Bluetooth model would pair with the revealing sound signature of the headphones. Usually Bluetooth compression is most obvious in the highest treble, where AT’s headphones shine. The ATH-M50xBT2 doesn’t pull any magic tricks here, but I’m pleased to report the Bluetooth compression doesn’t intrude as a distracting element of the listening experience.

If you can’t tell already, I have the curse. I’m plagued by a keen ear and an understanding of technology’s abilities and inadequacies.

Here are my takeaways regarding the ATH-M50xBT2, in comparison to the ATH-M40x:

- noticeably heavier in hand and on head, but not uncomfortably so

- spring rate of the headband is higher to compensate for the weight, leading to a more firm feeling headband flex. The clamping force on my head is proportionally higher than the lighter headphone, but not uncomfortable. If you are sensitive to clamping pressure or have an enormous head, the Bluetooth model may not be comfortable.

- high build quality for both headphones, but the Bluetooth model has a more lumpy headband. Maybe there are batteries stored up here?

- in 2021 (with an iPhone) Bluetooth + USB is more convenient than 3.5mm cord and lightning adapter

- excellent battery life to get through a full work week of use, and I don’t find the infrequent charging to be a big annoyance versus the ATH-M40x

- you can use any 3.5mm cable (with stepped plug) to connect in wired mode! No need for the proprietary twist-lock 2.5mm plug found on ATH-M40x. Plugging in a 3.5mm cable turns off Bluetooth and the headphones act in passive mode.

- highest frequency treble has better clarity on ATH-M40x. Tracks like “Sweet Jane” by Cowboy Junkies make this evident in the hiss of the cymbals. If you are looking for sonic differences due to Bluetooth compression, you’ll find it in specific instances of songs with crisp treble.

- the earcup material is slightly more grippy on the ATH-M50xBT2, and gives a little more friction to keep the heavier headphone in place. It also collects a little more dust than the ATH-M40x earcup material

- it’s nice being able to adjust volume and trigger Siri without removing my phone from my pocket, but this is a minor convenience compared to the major convenience of no cord.

- the earcups swivel 180° on the ATH-M50xBT2 versus 90° on the ATH-M40x. This makes no difference to me as they both sit comfortably on my ears and both fold up to the same size for travel

- ATH-M40x came with a really nice coiled/stretchy cable in addition to the long, straight cable. ATH-M50xBT2 only comes with a straight cable. I would definitely prefer the coiled cable but understand the decision to reduce cost on a product which will typically be used cordless.

- at work I like to connect to my personal phone for music, but plug into my company computer to join meetings. With the ATH-M40x I would remove the plug from my iPhone and plug it into my laptop. With the ATH-M50xBT2, I leave the plug in my laptop and simply plug the cable into my headphones to join a meeting. After a meeting I unplug the headphones and press the power button to go back to music. Note the earcup volume controls do not have any effect when the headphone is in corded mode!

- the ATH-M50xBT2 amplifier creates a small digital sound in the right earphone when powered on but no sound is playing. It sounds like electronic noise. I would prefer that it did not make this sound. However, once you begin playing audio, the sound either goes away or becomes imperceptible. It is not a problem in use, but a little quirk that I would prefer removed. Apparently the original ATH-M50xBT also had this behavior.

Overall, I’m happy with both pairs of headphones. I find myself using the ATH-M50xBT2 more often than the ATH-M40x due to the convenience, even though I am aware of the difference in treble reproduction due to Bluetooth compression.

I think these headphones are a much better value than AirPods Max (which also suffer from Bluetooth bandwidth limitations). If you’re happy to use your headphones with a cord, stick with the ATH-M40x.

Brand: Sony Aspects: anc, battery, build, comfort, connectivity, microphone, sound Rating: 5.0 Importance Score: 34.93 Review: A worthwhile successor to the incredible M3 with some upgrades we were waiting for!! It’s finally here and a worthwhile successor to the amazing M3. So I will start by saying that currently my go to headphones are the M3 but my favorite headphones over the last 5 years have been the Bose QC 35 II. These however are going to be my main pair especially for use doing work from home.
The battery seems to charge in about the same time and have the same life span which is around 30 hours and pretty incredible. So here is what I have noticed listening to the M3 versus the M4. I am glad they kept the M3 design which I personally like more than the Bose 700. The ear cushion feels a little more comfortable and is a little bigger making the comfortable M3 just a little more comfortable but there doesn’t seem to be a huge difference. Hopefully the padding holds up as well as the M3 has as mine still are incredible shape despite a lot of use. The M4 also changed the NC/Ambient button on the M3 to a Custom which for me was not really necessary but it will allow you to make that button programmable through the app to do what you want which maybe a welcome change for some users. There is also a proximity sensor inside the ear to support the new feature “Auto Pause” which I think can be really helpful. The idea is that once you take the earphones off it will immediately pause what you are listening too and that normally works great. However this did not work a few times when I lifted my earphones to the top of my head which makes sense because the sensor was reading my hair inside the cushion as if it was ear. I tend to shift my headphones this way at times when I take them off but when I took them completely off or had them around my neck it worked great. I like it better than using the touch pad outside the ear as honestly I have never used that feature that much because of it misreading my touch and skipping something I was watching. The touch pad is indeed back and for those who use it all the swipes seem to have the same function.
One big new feature with the M4 which I wonder if it could be added to the M3 via a firmware update is “Speak to Chat” which reads your voice and switches the earphones to transparency mode. Speak to chat is an interesting feature because it definitely has its uses if you are using the earphones for just noise cancelling when working at home or in the office so if a family member or co worker enters it allows you have to a brief exchange flawlessly. This is definitely helpful when working at home which many of us are doing these days. The downside is when listening to music if I decide to sing badly it switches me to transparency mode which is the last thing I want. So if you like to sing along to music you are listening to or if you ever talk to yourself while doing work “ Speak to chat” is not a feature for you. Luckily if you are not a fan it is easily disabled in the app.
My favorite new features by far though are the ability to finally pair your headphones to more than one device via Bluetooth which allows me to be connected to both a laptop and my phone. When a call comes in it auto switches you to the phone call and when it ends returns you right back to whatever you were listening. I believe there is a limit of 2 Bluetooth connections but this feature works well and is long overdue. This leads directly to my favorite upgrade which is the MIC handles phones calls much much better than the M3. There is a huge difference in call quality with the M4 that allows them to fix the M3s biggest flaw. I have taken calls on it and listened to someone else calling from the M4 and there is a HUGE difference from the M3.
I think there are slight improvements to the sound and noise cancellation quality but there is not a huge difference in my opinion. The sound can always be made even better by spending quality time with the app adjusting audio settings. Other features such as covering the right earphone to allow ambient noise pass through and the touch pad on it still have the same functionality.
The only feature that I still wish for is the ability to charge the M4 while using it (and a longer USB C charging cable pack in). This is a quality upgrade and for me the clarity of the phone calls thanks to the new additional microphone is a standout however if you have M3s and want to wait until the holidays for a sale I think that could make a lot of sense. If you were on the fence though and these new features are what you were waiting for you will enjoy these! They are in my opinion the best mass market noise cancelling headphones you can buy!

Brand: Sony Aspects: anc, build, comfort, connectivity, microphone, price, sound Rating: 4.0 Importance Score: 9.44 Review: Really PREMIUM sound! I've heard a lot of wonderful things about the Sony noise cancelling sets like these, and let me tell you: it isn't just hype. The sound quality is excellent, and the noise cancelling is eerily amazing. I put it on my wife and kids with the noise cancelling off and then turned it on to demonstrate, and you can see each them suddenly smile like "whoa". Like my Sony studio headphones I use professionally for work, the actual fidelity of the sound it reproduces is excellent, and the microphone for calls is excellent despite being up by my ears and so far from my mouth (other bluetooth headphones I've tried were good for music only, the mic being up by my ears wasn't good enough to isolate my voice from ambient sound, but this definitely does a great job).
The app gives a ton of control beyond what I expected to really customize the experience. For example, I have mine set to auto-pause if the headphones are removed, and to turn off noise cancelling when it detects me speaking to someone, and then resume playback when the headphones are removed/replaced. These features are all customizable, as well as many more. But you need the app. And this is where the cons come in:
1) You really need the app to get the most out of this. I was thinking about pairing this with my desktop and/or TV, but without the app you have far less control, and to be honest I couldn't even get it to pair with my phone using just Bluetooth alone. There seems to be a way according to the instructions, but I couldn't figure it out without using the app installed.
2) For a set that costs this much, I'm surprised at how scuffed up it got after one fall from about 3 feet next to my car. Including pictures. This happened the day I got it. I'm not sure if there's anything that can be done about that, pavement is hard to recover from if anything is dropped, but it's something to keep in mind if you're spending this much money on a gadget. My studio headphones have been roughed around for years and held up fine.

All in all, I'd give it 5 stars if it weren't so reliant on the app to get the features going and/or more resilient to being dropped. Feels like I should deduct one for that. But as far as features and sound quality go, this is a solid 5 out of 5.

Brand: SoundPEATS Aspects: anc, comfort, connectivity, microphone, sound Rating: 4.0 Importance Score: 6.96 Review: Nice for workouts Overall, I like these headphones a lot. I normally use Bose noise canceling earbuds (which have super comfy earpieces), and I think the sound of these compares quite favorably, and I now use these instead when I work out. I love not having to deal with the earphone cord anymore during my workout - I have gotten my arm caught in it while on the elliptical and jerked my phone to the floor.

The connecting cable goes behind your head, and it appears the earpieces are meant to hook over and behind your ears. I know this sounds strange, but you can totally just rest them on the outside of your ears instead of behind. Due to the coiled cable, they stay in place really well, so you can pretty much play with the placement to get the best fit for your ears. I chose to use the largest size of the three ear cushions that came with them - it gave the most cushioning and best fit. The cable length is perfect for me, but they were too tight for my grown son - not enough slack. I also like how they coil up when not in use - they fold compactly - I don't even use the case (though I probably should) - I just pop them into a pocket of my gym bag. They are pretty sturdy, and you don't feel like you're going to damage a fragile connecting wire.

The instructions are easy to follow, and I liked the placement of the controls on the right earbud. You do have to learn how to use the controls - the most confusing thing is that the controls are multi-function - they do different things depending on whether you use a long or short press. If you use them regularly, you'll quickly get the hang of it. I had no trouble pairing them with either an iphone 6 or watch. I tested them on a phone call, and they worked really well; the caller could not tell I wasn't on my phone. It was definitely very hands-free.

I know looks is probably not the biggest factor for headphones, but these are low-profile, and I like the black. I've tried two other bluetooth headphones, both white, with skinny connecting cables, and I much prefer the look, fit, and sound from these.

Brand: Audio-Technica Aspects: anc, build, comfort, connectivity, microphone, price, sound Rating: 5.0 Importance Score: 97.43 Review: Awesome Headphones… and How to Make Them Work with Apple or Android I am very pleased with these headphones. They sound great and seem very sturdy. At $129 from Amazon Deal of the Day, they were a real steal.

There is one issue, however: this pair comes with 3 different cables, yet none of them have an inline remote. So, if you’re using an Apple or Android device, such as an iPod/iPhone/iPad or Android Phone or tablet, you won’t have the convenience of controlling the volume or skipping tracks without reaching for you device. Also, you won’t have an inline mic, should you desire to use this pair as a headset.

This problem is easily fixable, though. If you look closely, the included cables have 2 types of plugs: a thin 2.5mm on one end (goes into the left headphone can) and a standard 3.5mm one on the other end (goes into your device’s audio jack). That happens to be the same configuration that is used in Bose QuietComfort 25 headphones. Luckily, Bose cables have inline remotes and mics, and it is easy to adapt those cables for use with Audio Technica ATH-M50X as well.

There is one caveat, however: ATH-M50X cable socket has a locking mechanism, thus the plug housing has 2 grooves on it. Bose QC25 cables lack that feature, but they can easily be modified to fit ATH-M50X. (See below.)

You have 2 choices: you can try and find a ready to use cable with inline remote, specific to your device, Apple or Android. (Keep in mind that they are different!) Alternatively, you can use a Bose QC25 cable and modify it a bit. “Genuine” Bose cables cost $30/ea, while aftermarket alternatives – only $15. As far as I can tell, they are pretty much the same when it comes to quality. (I have Bose QC25 headphones as well.)

Here are “ready to go” cables (Apple only):
http://www.amazon.com/Replacement-Technica-ATH-M50x-ATH-M40x-headphones/dp/B00NUJ4MLQ/“#”

Here are Bose QC25 aftermarket cables:
For Apple devices:
http://www.amazon.com/Compatible-Remote-QuiteComfort-Headphones-Features/dp/B0108QFIIO/

For Android Devices:
http://www.amazon.com/Android-Compatible-QuiteComfort-Headphones-Features/dp/B00YNUQPBQ/

If you get a Bose QC25 cable, here is what to do:
- Insert the plug of your new cable into the cable socket of the headphones and try to push it in a bit. (Do not rotate!) It will not go in all the way, but the locking mechanism within the socket will leave a couple of impressions on the cable plug housing. These will tell you where to cut the grooves and how deep to make them.
- Place the plug side by side with the plug from the original ATH-M50X cable and mark the lengths of the grooves to cut in it.
- Using an Xacto knife carefully create 2 grooves on both sides of the plug housing. Use the impressions from Step 1 for guidance. Do not make the grooves too deep, less than 1mm deep should be enough. In my case the modified plug sits tightly in the socket, so I chose not to cut the additional grooves to make the plug rotatable; it simply works as it is now, as it is made of rubber.

See the attached picture. The top plug is from an aftermarket cable, the bottom is from an ATH-M50x cable.

Enjoy!

Review: My new go to headphones. For years I have been buying $20 to $100 headphones in hope of finding one that shines beyond the price range. So far, I've enjoyed the open air Sennheiser PX-100 1 and 2. These sound much better then their price tag although it is not the headphone for those looking for a strong bass. I also tried other closed Sennheisers as well as the Bose AR at this price range and the Bose had too much bass and not enough balance for me. The last headphone I tried was the ATH-M30X and these were fine but I preferred the Senheiser open air headphones. I finally decided to buy a headphone in the next price range $100 to $200 to see if it made a difference. Fortunately, the ATHM50X was the first one I tried given the positive reviews. Let me tell you that there is a difference in quality and balance that is evident even to the casual listener (me). The clarity and balance of appropriate bass and treble blows away anything else I've tried. So if you are on the fence on this give it a try and if you are like me this will be your new go to headphones. The only problem now is that I may be tempted to go to the next price range of headphones. Bottom line - this is the headphone for this and any price range below for me.
